title: 90.	DP on trees: Tree diameter with weights (DP style).
description: 90.	DP on trees: Tree diameter with weights (DP style).

examples.input: Tree with 3 nodes: edges (0-1 weight 2), (1-2 weight 3)
examples.explanation: Adjacency list: {0: [(1,2)], 1: [(0,2), (2,3)], 2: [(1,3)]}. Start DFS from node 0: visited reset. Stack: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0), mark visited, max_diam=0. Push (1,0+2=2). Pop (1,2), mark visited, max_diam=2. Push (0,2+2=4) but 0 visited, skip; push (2,2+3=5). Pop (2,5), mark visited, max_diam=5. Stack empty. DFS from node 1: visited reset. Stack: [(1,0)]. Pop (1,0), mark visited, max_diam=5 (unchanged). Push (0,0+2=2), (2,0+3=3). Pop (2,3), mark visited, max_diam=5. Push (1,3+3=6) but 1 visited, skip. Pop (0,2), mark visited, max_diam=5. Push (1,2+2=4) but 1 visited, skip. DFS from node 2: similar, max_diam stays 5. Final max_diam=5, path 0-1-2 with sum 2+3=5.

examples.input: Tree with 4 nodes: edges (0-1 weight 1), (1-2 weight 2), (2-3 weight 1)
examples.explanation: Adjacency list: {0: [(1,1)], 1: [(0,1), (2,2)], 2: [(1,2), (3,1)], 3: [(2,1)]}. Start DFS from node 0: visited reset. Stack: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0), mark visited, max_diam=0. Push (1,1). Pop (1,1), mark visited, max_diam=1. Push (0,1+1=2) skip; push (2,1+2=3). Pop (2,3), mark visited, max_diam=3. Push (1,3+2=5) skip; push (3,3+1=4). Pop (3,4), mark visited, max_diam=4. DFS from other nodes: from node 1, max path 1-2-3 weight 3, but max_diam stays 4; from node 2, path 2-1-0 weight 3; from node 3, path 3-2-1-0 weight 4. Final max_diam=4, path 0-1-2-3 or 3-2-1-0 with sum 1+2+1=4.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²): All Paths DFS
approaches.summary: Perform DFS from every node to compute all possible paths, tracking the maximum weighted path length. This explores all node pairs explicitly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Tree Diameter with Weights via All-Pairs DFS
ladders.desc: Compute the longest weighted path in a tree by performing DFS from every node to explore all possible paths.
ladders.explanation: The tree diameter problem with weighted edges asks for the maximum sum of edge weights along any simple path in the tree. A brute-force approach involves treating each node as a potential starting point and performing DFS to find the farthest reachable node from it, recording the maximum path length encountered. Since a tree has exactly one simple path between any two nodes, we can compute all pairwise distances by starting DFS from every node. The intuition is that the diameter must be between some pair of nodes, so by checking all pairs, we guarantee finding it. The algorithm: 1) Represent the tree as an adjacency list with weights. 2) For each node u, run DFS to compute distances to all other nodes v. 3) During DFS, maintain a visited set to avoid cycles (though trees are acyclic, this prevents revisiting). 4) Track the maximum distance found across all DFS calls. Time complexity is O(n²) because from each of n nodes, DFS visits all other nodes. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and adjacency list. Connection to L1: This uses the DFS subroutine from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes include not handling weighted edges correctly (summing weights instead of counting edges), forgetting to reset visited states between DFS calls, and inefficiently recomputing paths. Real code pattern: def tree_diameter_brute_force(adj_list): n = len(adj_list); max_diam = 0; for start in range(n): visited = [False]*n; stack = [(start, 0)]; while stack: node, dist = stack.pop(); if not visited[node]: visited[node] = True; max_diam = max(max_diam, dist); for neighbor, weight in adj_list[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: stack.append((neighbor, dist + weight)); return max_diam.

examples.input: Tree with 3 nodes: edges (0-1 weight 2), (1-2 weight 3)
examples.trace: Adjacency list: {0: [(1,2)], 1: [(0,2), (2,3)], 2: [(1,3)]}. Start DFS from node 0: visited reset. Stack: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0), mark visited, max_diam=0. Push (1,0+2=2). Pop (1,2), mark visited, max_diam=2. Push (0,2+2=4) but 0 visited, skip; push (2,2+3=5). Pop (2,5), mark visited, max_diam=5. Stack empty. DFS from node 1: visited reset. Stack: [(1,0)]. Pop (1,0), mark visited, max_diam=5 (unchanged). Push (0,0+2=2), (2,0+3=3). Pop (2,3), mark visited, max_diam=5. Push (1,3+3=6) but 1 visited, skip. Pop (0,2), mark visited, max_diam=5. Push (1,2+2=4) but 1 visited, skip. DFS from node 2: similar, max_diam stays 5. Final max_diam=5, path 0-1-2 with sum 2+3=5.

examples.input: Tree with 4 nodes: edges (0-1 weight 1), (1-2 weight 2), (2-3 weight 1)
examples.trace: Adjacency list: {0: [(1,1)], 1: [(0,1), (2,2)], 2: [(1,2), (3,1)], 3: [(2,1)]}. Start DFS from node 0: visited reset. Stack: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0), mark visited, max_diam=0. Push (1,1). Pop (1,1), mark visited, max_diam=1. Push (0,1+1=2) skip; push (2,1+2=3). Pop (2,3), mark visited, max_diam=3. Push (1,3+2=5) skip; push (3,3+1=4). Pop (3,4), mark visited, max_diam=4. DFS from other nodes: from node 1, max path 1-2-3 weight 3, but max_diam stays 4; from node 2, path 2-1-0 weight 3; from node 3, path 3-2-1-0 weight 4. Final max_diam=4, path 0-1-2-3 or 3-2-1-0 with sum 1+2+1=4.

Tree with 4 nodes: edges (0-1 weight 3), (1-2 weight 4), (2-3 weight 2)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS from a Single Source to Find Farthest Node
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from a given start node to compute the maximum distance to any other node in the tree.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the brute-force approach: from a fixed start node, explore all reachable nodes via DFS to find the farthest one in terms of edge weight sum. The intuition is that in a tree, DFS naturally traverses all nodes without cycles, allowing us to accumulate path weights. The algorithm: 1) Input: adjacency list with weights and a start node. 2) Initialize a visited array (size n) to false and a stack with (start, 0) where 0 is the current distance. 3) While stack is not empty, pop a node and its distance. If not visited, mark it visited and update a global max_distance. 4) For each neighbor with weight, if not visited, push (neighbor, current distance + weight). 5) Return max_distance. Time complexity is O(n) as each node and edge is visited once. Space complexity is O(n) for stack and visited. Connection to L0: This is called n times in L0 to cover all start nodes. Common mistakes include not using visited (causing infinite loops in general graphs, though trees are safe), incorrect weight accumulation, or using BFS which works but DFS is simpler here. Real code pattern: def dfs_farthest(adj_list, start): n = len(adj_list); visited = [False]*n; stack = [(start, 0)]; max_dist = 0; while stack: node, dist = stack.pop(); if not visited[node]: visited[node] = True; max_dist = max(max_dist, dist); for neighbor, weight in adj_list[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: stack.append((neighbor, dist + weight)); return max_dist.

examples.input: Tree: edges (0-1 weight 2), (1-2 weight 3); start node 0
examples.trace: Adjacency list: {0: [(1,2)], 1: [(0,2), (2,3)], 2: [(1,3)]}. Start at 0 with dist 0. Stack: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0), mark visited, max_dist=0. Push (1,0+2=2). Pop (1,2), mark visited, max_dist=2. Push (0,2+2=4) but 0 visited, skip; push (2,2+3=5). Pop (2,5), mark visited, max_dist=5. Stack empty. Return 5.

examples.input: Tree: edges (0-1 weight 1), (1-2 weight 2), (2-3 weight 1); start node 1
examples.trace: Adjacency list: {0: [(1,1)], 1: [(0,1), (2,2)], 2: [(1,2), (3,1)], 3: [(2,1)]}. Start at 1 with dist 0. Stack: [(1,0)]. Pop (1,0), mark visited, max_dist=0. Push (0,0+1=1), (2,0+2=2). Pop (2,2), mark visited, max_dist=2. Push (1,2+2=4) skip; push (3,2+1=3). Pop (3,3), mark visited, max_dist=3. Pop (0,1), mark visited, max_dist=3. Push (1,1+1=2) skip. Return 3.

testCases.input: Tree: edges (0-1 weight 5), (1-2 weight 1), (2-3 weight 2); start 1
Tree: edges (0-1 weight 1), (1-2 weight 2), (2-3 weight 3); start 3
Tree: edges (0-1 weight 2), (0-2 weight 3), (0-3 weight 1); start 2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Accumulating Path Weights During Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Sum edge weights along a path during DFS to compute distances between nodes.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is to traverse the tree while maintaining a running total of edge weights from the start node. This allows computing the weighted distance to each visited node. The intuition is that in a tree, each edge is traversed exactly once in a DFS from a source, so we can add its weight to a cumulative sum as we move deeper. The algorithm: 1) Start at a node with initial distance 0. 2) When moving from node u to neighbor v via edge weight w, the distance to v becomes distance_to_u + w. 3) Use a stack or recursion to propagate this sum. 4) Keep track of the maximum sum encountered. Time complexity per DFS is O(n), space O(n). Connection to L1: This weight accumulation is embedded in the DFS subroutine. Common mistakes include resetting the distance incorrectly when backtracking (not needed in this iterative approach), confusing weight with node count, or not handling negative weights (though typically non-negative in this problem). Real code pattern: In DFS, when pushing a neighbor: stack.append((neighbor, current_dist + weight)).

examples.input: Path: nodes 0-1-2 with weights 2 and 3; start at 0
examples.trace: Start: current_dist=0 at node 0. Move to node 1: current_dist=0+2=2. At node 1: record dist 2. Move to node 2: current_dist=2+3=5. At node 2: record dist 5. No more moves. Max cumulative is 5.

examples.input: Star: center 0, edges to 1,2,3 with weights 1,2,3; start at 0
examples.trace: Start: current_dist=0 at node 0. Move to node 1: current_dist=0+1=1. Record 1. Back to 0, reset current_dist=0. Move to node 2: current_dist=0+2=2. Record 2. Back to 0, reset. Move to node 3: current_dist=0+3=3. Record 3. Max cumulative is 3.

testCases.input: Star: center 0, edges to 1,2 with weights 5,6; start 0

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative DFS on a Weighted Tree
ladders.desc: Traverse all nodes of a tree using a stack, handling weighted edges.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves performing a depth-first search on a tree represented as a weighted graph, using an explicit stack to avoid recursion limits. The intuition is that DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, and with weights, we associate each edge with a cost. The algorithm: 1) Input: adjacency list where each entry is a list of (neighbor, weight). 2) Start from a given node, push it onto a stack with initial distance 0. 3) While stack not empty, pop a node. If not visited, mark visited and process it. 4) For each neighbor, if not visited, push (neighbor, current distance + weight). 5) Continue until stack empty. Time O(n), space O(n). Connection to L2: This traversal enables weight accumulation. Common mistakes include using a queue (BFS) which changes traversal order but still works, forgetting to mark visited before pushing neighbors (risking duplicates), or mishandling weights in the stack. Real code pattern: stack = [(start, 0)]; visited = [False]*n; while stack: node, dist = stack.pop(); if not visited[node]: visited[node] = True; for neighbor, weight in adj_list[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: stack.append((neighbor, dist + weight)).

examples.input: Tree: edges (0-1 weight 1), (1-2 weight 2); start 0
examples.trace: Stack: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0), mark visited. Push (1,0+1=1). Stack: [(1,1)]. Pop (1,1), mark visited. Push (0,1+1=2) but 0 visited, skip; push (2,1+2=3). Stack: [(2,3)]. Pop (2,3), mark visited. Push (1,3+2=5) skip. Stack empty. Order visited: 0,1,2.

examples.input: Tree: edges (0-1 weight 3), (0-2 weight 4); start 0
examples.output: Traversal order: 0, then 1 or 2 depending on stack order, with distances 0,3,4
examples.trace: Stack: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0), mark visited. Push (1,0+3=3), (2,0+4=4). Suppose stack LIFO: pop (2,4) next, mark visited. Push (0,4+4=8) skip. Then pop (1,3), mark visited. Push (0,3+3=6) skip. Order: 0,2,1 or 0,1,2.

testCases.input: Tree: edges (0-1 weight 3), (1-2 weight 4), (2-3 weight 5); start 0
Tree: edges (0-1 weight 2), (0-2 weight 3), (1-3 weight 1); start 0
Tree: edges (0-1 weight 1), (1-2 weight 2), (1-3 weight 3); start 1
Tree: edges (0-1 weight 4), (1-2 weight 5), (2-3 weight 6); start 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Representing a Weighted Tree as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Create a data structure to store tree nodes and weighted edges for efficient traversal.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves building an adjacency list representation of a weighted tree. A tree is an undirected graph with n nodes and n-1 edges, no cycles. The adjacency list is a list of lists (or dictionary) where each index corresponds to a node, and contains pairs (neighbor, weight) for each connected edge. The intuition is that this allows O(1) access to neighbors and their weights, crucial for DFS. Steps: 1) Given number of nodes n and list of edges as (u, v, weight). 2) Initialize adj_list as a list of empty lists of size n. 3) For each edge (u, v, w), append (v, w) to adj_list[u] and (u, w) to adj_list[v] since tree is undirected. 4) Ensure no duplicates (each edge appears once in input). Time O(n) to build, space O(n). Connection to L3: This data structure is input for iterative DFS. Common mistakes include forgetting to add both directions for undirected edges, using incorrect indices, or not handling weights properly. Real code pattern: n = 5; edges = [(0,1,2), (1,2,3)]; adj_list = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v,w in edges: adj_list[u].append((v,w)); adj_list[v].append((u,w)).

examples.trace: Initialize adj_list of size 3: [[], [], []]. Process edge (0,1,2): append (1,2) to index 0; append (0,2) to index 1. Now adj_list: [[(1,2)], [(0,2)], []]. Process edge (1,2,3): append (2,3) to index 1; append (1,3) to index 2. Final: [[(1,2)], [(0,2), (2,3)], [(1,3)]].
Initialize size 2: [[], []]. Process edge (0,1,5): append (1,5) to index 0; append (0,5) to index 1. Final: [[(1,5)], [(0,5)]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Trees and Simple Paths
ladders.desc: Learn basic tree properties: nodes, edges, and simple paths without cycles.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a tree is an undirected graph with n nodes and exactly n-1 edges, containing no cycles. A simple path is a sequence of nodes where each adjacent pair is connected by an edge, and no node is repeated. The intuition is that in a tree, there is exactly one simple path between any two nodes, which is key to the diameter problem. Concepts: 1) Node: a point in the tree. 2) Edge: connection between two nodes, with a weight (a number). 3) Path length: sum of weights of edges in the path. 4) Diameter: the longest simple path in the tree. For beginners, visualize a tree as a hierarchy like a family tree or folder structure. Example: a tree with nodes 0,1,2 and edges 0-1 and 1-2: paths include 0-1 (length weight of edge), 1-2, and 0-1-2 (sum of both weights). Connection to L4: This knowledge informs how to build the adjacency list. Common mistakes include confusing trees with general graphs (allowing cycles) or misunderstanding path uniqueness. Real code pattern: No code here, just conceptual understanding.

examples.input: Tree: nodes 0,1,2; edges 0-1 weight 2, 1-2 weight 3
examples.trace: List all node pairs: (0,1): path 0-1, length 2. (1,2): path 1-2, length 3. (0,2): only path is 0-1-2, length 2+3=5. No other paths. Longest is 5.

examples.input: Tree: nodes 0,1,2,3; edges 0-1 weight 1, 1-2 weight 2, 2-3 weight 1
examples.trace: Consider path from 0 to 3: must go 0-1-2-3, length 1+2+1=4. Other paths: 0-1 length 1, 1-2 length 2, etc. Longest is 4.

testCases.input: Tree: nodes 0,1,2,3; edges 0-1 weight 2, 0-2 weight 3, 0-3 weight 4
testCases.expected: Paths: 0-1 length 2, 0-2 length 3, 0-3 length 4, 1-0-2 length 5, etc.; diameter 7 (e.g., 1-0-3 length 6 or 2-0-3 length 7)

testCases.input: Tree: nodes 0,1,2,3,4; chain edges all weight 1

testCases.expected: No edges; only path is trivial length 0; diameter 0

testCases.input: Tree: nodes 0,1,2,3; edges 0-1 weight 3, 1-2 weight 4, 2-3 weight 5
Tree: nodes 0,1,2,3; edges 0-1 weight 1, 1-2 weight 2, 1-3 weight 3

approaches.name: Two DFS O(n): Farthest Node Method
approaches.summary: First DFS finds the farthest node from an arbitrary start, second DFS from that farthest node finds the diameter. Handles weighted edges by summing weights during traversal.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Tree Diameter with Weighted Edges Using Two DFS
ladders.desc: Find the longest path (sum of edge weights) between any two nodes in a weighted tree using two DFS traversals.
ladders.explanation: The two-DFS approach efficiently computes the diameter of a weighted tree in O(n) time. The key insight is that in a tree, the farthest node from an arbitrary starting point must be one endpoint of the diameter. The first DFS from any node (typically node 0) finds this farthest endpoint by tracking cumulative distances. The second DFS starts from this farthest node to find the actual diameter length, as the farthest distance from this endpoint will be the diameter. This works because trees have no cycles, ensuring that the longest path is uniquely determined by its endpoints. The algorithm handles weighted edges by summing weights during DFS traversal instead of counting edges. We maintain a distance array to track the maximum distance from the current start node to all other nodes. The DFS recursively explores all children, updating distances with edge weights. After the first DFS, we identify the node with maximum distance. The second DFS from that node gives the diameter as the maximum distance found. Time complexity is O(n) for two DFS traversals, each visiting n nodes once. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and distance storage. Connection to L1: This full solution implements the farthest node method by calling two DFS traversals sequentially. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs (trees are connected by definition), incorrect weight accumulation, or using BFS which works but DFS is more natural for tree recursion. Real code pattern uses adjacency list with (neighbor, weight) pairs and a visited set to prevent cycles.

examples.input: Tree with 4 nodes: edges: 0-1 weight 2, 1-2 weight 3, 1-3 weight 1
examples.trace: Step 1: First DFS from node 0. Initialize distances = [0, -inf, -inf, -inf]. DFS(0): Visit neighbor 1 with weight 2, update distance[1] = 2. DFS(1): Visit neighbor 2 with weight 3, update distance[2] = 2+3 = 5. Visit neighbor 3 with weight 1, update distance[3] = 2+1 = 3. Distances: [0, 2, 5, 3]. Farthest node = 2 with distance 5. Step 2: Second DFS from node 2. Reset distances = [0, -inf, -inf, -inf], set distance[2] = 0. DFS(2): Visit neighbor 1 with weight 3, update distance[1] = 3. DFS(1): Visit neighbor 0 with weight 2, update distance[0] = 3+2 = 5. Visit neighbor 3 with weight 1, update distance[3] = 3+1 = 4. Distances: [5, 3, 0, 4]. Maximum distance = 5. Diameter = 5.

examples.input: Tree with 5 nodes: edges: 0-1 weight 4, 0-2 weight 2, 1-3 weight 1, 2-4 weight 3
examples.trace: Step 1: First DFS from node 0. Distances start: [0, -inf, -inf, -inf, -inf]. DFS(0): Visit neighbor 1 weight 4 → distance[1]=4. Visit neighbor 2 weight 2 → distance[2]=2. DFS(1): Visit neighbor 3 weight 1 → distance[3]=4+1=5. DFS(2): Visit neighbor 4 weight 3 → distance[4]=2+3=5. Distances: [0,4,2,5,5]. Farthest nodes: 3 or 4 (distance 5). Pick node 3. Step 2: Second DFS from node 3. Reset distances: [0, -inf, -inf, -inf, -inf], distance[3]=0. DFS(3): Visit neighbor 1 weight 1 → distance[1]=1. DFS(1): Visit neighbor 0 weight 4 → distance[0]=1+4=5. Visit neighbor 2? No direct edge. DFS(0): Visit neighbor 2 weight 2 → distance[2]=5+2=7. DFS(2): Visit neighbor 4 weight 3 → distance[4]=7+3=10. Wait, correction: Actually from node 0, we go to node 2: distance[2] = distance[0]+2 = 5+2=7. Then from node 2 to 4: distance[4] = distance[2]+3 = 7+3=10. But tree paths are unique: Path from 3 to 4 is 3-1-0-2-4 with weights 1+4+2+3=10. Distances: [5,1,7,0,10]. Maximum distance = 10. Diameter = 10. Re-check: Actually longest path is between nodes 3 and 4: path 3-1-0-2-4, sum weights 1+4+2+3=10. But wait, that's 10, not 9. Let me recalc carefully. Tree: edges: 0-1(4), 0-2(2), 1-3(1), 2-4(3). All paths: 3-1-0-2-4: 1+4+2+3=10. Other paths: 3-1-0: 1+4=5, 4-2-0: 3+2=5, 3-1-0-2: 1+4+2=7, 4-2-0-1: 3+2+4=9. So diameter is actually 10, not 9. Correct output: 10. Trace update: In step 2, distances become [5,1,7,0,10], max=10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS to Find Farthest Node and Distance
ladders.desc: Perform a DFS from a given start node to compute distances to all nodes and return the farthest node and maximum distance.
ladders.explanation: This DFS subroutine is the core of both traversals in the two-DFS method. Given a start node in a weighted tree, it recursively explores all nodes, accumulating edge weights to compute the distance from the start to each node. The algorithm uses a visited set to avoid cycles (though trees are acyclic, this prevents re-visiting parents). It maintains a distance array where distance[u] stores the cumulative weight from start to node u. The DFS begins at the start node with distance 0. For each neighbor, if not visited, it updates the neighbor's distance as current distance plus edge weight, then recurses. After full traversal, it finds the node with maximum distance—this is the farthest node from the start. The intuition is that DFS naturally explores all paths in a tree, and by summing weights, we get the weighted distance. This works because trees have unique paths between nodes, so DFS covers each path exactly once. Time complexity is O(n) as each node and edge is visited once. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and distance array. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called twice in the full solution—first to find a diameter endpoint, then to measure the diameter. Connection to L2: This implements the farthest node concept by actually computing distances. Common mistakes include not resetting visited set between calls, incorrect weight addition, or using BFS (which also works but DFS is shown here). Real code pattern uses recursion with adjacency list.

examples.input: Start node=0, tree: edges 0-1 weight 3, 1-2 weight 4
examples.trace: Initialize visited={}, distances=[0,-inf,-inf]. DFS(0): distance[0]=0. Visit neighbor 1: weight 3, not visited. Update distance[1]=0+3=3. DFS(1): Visit neighbor 2: weight 4, update distance[2]=3+4=7. Visit neighbor 0: already visited, skip. Distances: [0,3,7]. Farthest node = 2 with distance 7.

examples.input: Start node=2, tree: edges 0-1 weight 1, 0-2 weight 2, 1-3 weight 3
examples.trace: Start node=2. Initialize distances: [0,-inf,-inf,-inf], set distance[2]=0. DFS(2): Visit neighbor 0 weight 2, update distance[0]=0+2=2. DFS(0): Visit neighbor 1 weight 1, update distance[1]=2+1=3. Visit neighbor 2: visited, skip. DFS(1): Visit neighbor 3 weight 3, update distance[3]=3+3=6. Visit neighbor 0: visited, skip. Distances: [2,3,0,6]. Farthest node = 3, max distance=6.

Start=2, tree: 5 nodes: edges 0-1 weight 7, 1-2 weight 1, 2-3 weight 2, 3-4 weight 3
Start=0, tree: 7 nodes: edges 0-1 weight 1, 1-2 weight 1, 2-3 weight 1, 3-4 weight 1, 4-5 weight 1, 5-6 weight 1
Start=5, tree: 8 nodes: edges 0-1 weight 2, 0-2 weight 3, 1-3 weight 4, 1-4 weight 1, 2-5 weight 5, 5-6 weight 2, 6-7 weight 3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Farthest Node from Any Start is a Diameter Endpoint
ladders.desc: Prove and apply the property that in a tree, the farthest node from an arbitrary node is always an endpoint of the diameter.
ladders.explanation: This core insight enables the two-DFS algorithm. In any tree (weighted or unweighted), if you pick any node X and find the node Y farthest from X (by sum of edge weights), then Y must be one endpoint of the tree's diameter (the longest path). The proof uses contradiction and tree properties. Suppose diameter endpoints are A and B, and Y is farthest from X but not an endpoint. Then consider paths: distance(X,Y) > distance(X,A) and distance(X,Y) > distance(X,B). By triangle inequality in trees (unique paths), this leads to a contradiction that the path A-B is not the longest. Intuitively, because trees have no cycles, the farthest point from any node will lie on the extreme end of the tree. This property reduces finding diameter to two steps: 1) Find an endpoint Y by DFS from any X. 2) Find the farthest node from Y, which gives the diameter length. This works for weighted edges because distances are sums of weights, not just edge counts. The logic holds as long as edge weights are non-negative (typical in such problems). Time/space: This insight doesn't add complexity but guides algorithm design. Connection to L1: This property justifies why the DFS subroutine finds a diameter endpoint. Connection to L3: It relies on tree properties like acyclicity. Common mistakes include assuming it works for graphs with cycles (it doesn't) or not handling negative weights (would break the property). Real code pattern: After first DFS, use argmax on distances to get Y.

examples.input: Tree: line of 3 nodes with equal weights 1, arbitrary start X=1
examples.output: Farthest node from X=1 is either 0 or 2, both are diameter endpoints
examples.trace: Tree: nodes 0-1-2, edges weight 1 each. Diameter endpoints: 0 and 2 (path length 2). Start X=1. Distances: from 1 to 0=1, to 2=1. Farthest nodes: 0 and 2 (distance 1). Both are indeed diameter endpoints. Property holds.

examples.input: Tree: star with center 0, leaves 1,2,3 all weight 2, start X=2
examples.output: Farthest node from X=2 is 0, which is a diameter endpoint? Check: diameter endpoints are any two leaves, e.g., 1 and 3, length 4. But farthest from X=2 is center 0 (distance 2), not a leaf. Wait, is 0 a diameter endpoint? Diameter is between two leaves, say 1 and 3: path 1-0-3, length 2+2=4. Endpoints are 1 and 3. But from X=2, farthest node is 0 (distance 2), not 1 or 3 (distance 4? Actually from 2 to 1: path 2-0-1, length 2+2=4, same as to 3). So farthest nodes from X=2 are 1 and 3 (distance 4), not 0. Let me recalc: Distances from node 2: to 0=2, to 1=2+2=4, to 3=2+2=4. So farthest are 1 and 3, which are diameter endpoints. Property holds.

testCases.input: Tree: two nodes weight 3, start X=0
Tree: line 4 nodes weights 1, start X=2
Tree: star center 0, leaves 1,2,3 weight 5, start X=1
Tree: line 5 nodes weights 2, start X=1

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal with Weighted Edge Accumulation
ladders.desc: Traverse a tree using DFS while summing edge weights to compute distances from a source node.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on traversing a tree and accumulating weights along paths. Unlike simple traversal that counts nodes or edges, here we maintain a running sum of edge weights as we move from node to node. The tree is represented as an adjacency list where each edge has a weight. DFS is used because it naturally explores deep paths before backtracking, which aligns with computing cumulative distances. Start at a source node with distance 0. For each neighbor, if not visited, add the edge weight to the current distance to get the neighbor's distance, mark visited, and recurse. This computes the distance from source to every other node. The key intuition is that in a tree, the path between any two nodes is unique, so DFS will follow each path exactly once, correctly summing weights. This is foundational for the farthest node computation. Time complexity is O(n) visiting each node once. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and visited set. Connection to L2: This traversal implements the distance computation needed to find farthest nodes. Connection to L4: It uses basic DFS and addition operations. Common mistakes include forgetting to pass cumulative distance recursively, incorrect visited handling leading to infinite loops, or not handling disconnected graphs (but trees are connected). Real code pattern: recursive function dfs(node, current_dist) updates distances and recurses on children.

examples.input: Source=0, tree: edges 0-1 weight 5, 0-2 weight 3
examples.trace: Start at node 0 with dist=0. Mark visited. For neighbor 1: weight 5, not visited. Compute dist for node1 = 0+5 = 5. Recurse dfs(1,5). Node 1 has neighbor 0 (visited) so skip. Backtrack. For neighbor 2: weight 3, dist for node2 = 0+3 = 3. Recurse dfs(2,3). Node 2 has neighbor 0 visited. Done. Distances: [0,5,3].

examples.input: Source=2, tree: edges 0-1 weight 2, 1-2 weight 4, 2-3 weight 1
examples.trace: Start at node 2 dist=0. Mark visited. Neighbor 1: weight 4, dist node1 = 0+4=4. Recurse dfs(1,4). Node 1 neighbors: 0 weight 2, dist node0=4+2=6; neighbor 2 visited skip. Recurse dfs(0,6). Node 0 neighbor 1 visited. Backtrack. Neighbor 3: weight 1, dist node3=0+1=1. Recurse dfs(3,1). Node 3 neighbor 2 visited. Distances: [6,4,0,1].

testCases.input: Source=0, tree: 4 nodes star weights all 4
Source=0, tree: 5 nodes: edges 0-1 weight 10, 0-2 weight 5, 1-3 weight 2, 2-4 weight 1
Source=2, tree: 7 nodes: edges 0-1 weight 1, 1-2 weight 2, 2-3 weight 3, 3-4 weight 4, 4-5 weight 5, 5-6 weight 6
Source=0, tree: 8 nodes: edges 0-1 weight 2, 0-2 weight 3, 1-3 weight 4, 1-4 weight 1, 2-5 weight 5, 5-6 weight 2, 6-7 weight 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) on a Tree
ladders.desc: Traverse all nodes of a tree using DFS, visiting each node exactly once.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. In a tree, which is a connected acyclic graph, DFS ensures each node is visited exactly once. The algorithm starts at a root node, marks it as visited, then recursively visits all unvisited neighbors. For trees, we don't need to worry about cycles, but we still use a visited set to avoid revisiting the parent. The intuition is to go deep first, which naturally explores paths from the root to leaves. This is implemented recursively: function dfs(node): mark visited, for each neighbor in adjacency_list[node], if neighbor not visited, call dfs(neighbor). This visits all nodes in O(n) time and O(n) space for recursion stack. Connection to L3: DFS is the traversal method used to accumulate weights. Connection to L5: It builds on tree representation. Common mistakes include infinite recursion if visited not used (though trees are acyclic, it's good practice), stack overflow for deep trees (can use iterative DFS), or incorrect adjacency list. Real code pattern: adjacency list as list of lists, visited set, recursive calls.

examples.input: Tree: edges 0-1, 0-2, start DFS at node 0
examples.trace: Start at node 0, mark visited. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Suppose we pick neighbor 1 first: visit node 1, mark visited. Node 1's neighbor is 0 (visited), so backtrack. Then from node 0, neighbor 2: visit node 2, mark visited. Node 2's neighbor 0 visited. Done. Order: 0,1,2.

examples.input: Tree: edges 0-1, 1-2, 1-3, start DFS at node 0
examples.trace: Start node 0, visited. Neighbor 1: visit node 1. From node 1, neighbors: 0 visited, 2 and 3 unvisited. Visit node 2. Node 2 neighbor 1 visited, backtrack. Visit node 3. Node 3 neighbor 1 visited. Order: 0,1,2,3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Tree Representation and Basic Properties
ladders.desc: Understand what a tree is, how to represent it, and its basic properties like connectivity and acyclicity.
ladders.explanation: A tree is a connected, acyclic graph with n nodes and n-1 edges. It has no cycles, meaning there is exactly one unique path between any two nodes. This property is crucial for algorithms like tree diameter. Trees can be rooted (with a designated root) or unrooted; for diameter, we treat them as unrooted. Common representations include adjacency list: for each node, a list of its neighbors. For weighted trees, each neighbor entry includes a weight. For example, adjacency_list[0] = [(1,5), (2,3)] means node 0 is connected to node 1 with weight 5 and node 2 with weight 3. Basic properties: connectivity ensures all nodes are reachable, acyclicity prevents infinite loops during traversal. These properties guarantee that DFS or BFS will visit all nodes exactly once. Understanding this foundation is essential before implementing traversals. Time/space: Representation uses O(n) space. Connection to L4: This representation is used in DFS traversal. Common mistakes: confusing trees with general graphs, incorrect adjacency list construction, or assuming directedness (trees are usually undirected). Real code pattern: adjacency_list = [[] for _ in range(n)], then for each edge (u,v,w): adjacency_list[u].append((v,w)), adjacency_list[v].append((u,w)).

examples.input: Tree with 3 nodes: edges 0-1 weight 2, 1-2 weight 3
examples.trace: Node 0 connected to node 1 weight 2 → add (1,2) to list of 0, and (0,2) to list of 1. Node 1 connected to node 2 weight 3 → add (2,3) to list of 1, and (1,3) to list of 2. Result: index0: [(1,2)], index1: [(0,2),(2,3)], index2: [(1,3)].

examples.input: Tree with 4 nodes: edges 0-1 weight 1, 0-2 weight 4, 0-3 weight 2
examples.trace: Node 0 has three edges: to 1 weight1, to2 weight4, to3 weight2. So list for 0: [(1,1),(2,4),(3,2)]. Node 1 only connected to 0: [(0,1)]. Similarly for nodes 2 and 3.

testCases.input: Tree: 5 nodes: edges 0-1 w4,0-2 w3,1-3 w2,2-4 w1
Tree: 7 nodes: star center 0 weights 1 to 6

approaches.name: DP on Tree O(n): Postorder DFS
approaches.summary: Perform postorder DFS, at each node compute the longest path through it as sum of two longest child paths. Maintain global maximum for diameter.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Tree Diameter with Weights (DP on Tree)
ladders.desc: Find the longest path between any two nodes in a weighted tree using postorder DFS DP approach.
ladders.explanation: The tree diameter problem with weights asks us to find the maximum distance between any two nodes in a weighted tree. The DP on tree approach works by performing a postorder DFS traversal. At each node, we compute the longest path from that node to a leaf in its subtree (height). The key insight is that the diameter passing through a node equals the sum of the two longest heights from its children plus their edge weights. We maintain a global maximum diameter as we process nodes. Intuition: The diameter must pass through some node as its highest point (like the peak of a mountain). By computing at each node the best path through it (using the two deepest subtrees), we guarantee finding the global maximum. Algorithm: 1. Initialize global diameter = 0. 2. Perform DFS from root. 3. For each node, compute heights from all children: height = max(child_height + edge_weight). 4. Track the two largest heights. 5. Update diameter = max(diameter, sum_of_two_largest_heights). 6. Return node's height = largest_height (or 0 if leaf). Time: O(n) visiting each node once. Space: O(n) for recursion stack. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into complete solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting edge weights in height calculation, not handling negative weights correctly (problem usually assumes non-negative), not resetting two largest heights per node. Real pattern: def dfs(node): heights = [dfs(child) + weight for child,weight]; top2 = nlargest(2, heights); diameter = max(diameter, sum(top2)); return max(heights, default=0).

examples.trace: DFS postorder: Node4: height=0, Node5: height=0, Node2: heights from children: 0+1=1 (from 4), 0+3=3 (from 5). Top2: [3,1], sum=4. diameter=4. Node2 height=3. Node3: height=0. Node1: heights: 3+4=7 (from 2), 0+2=2 (from 3). Top2: [7,2], sum=9. diameter=max(4,9)=9. But wait, check: Path 5-2-1-3: 3+4+2=9? Actually edges: 5-2 weight3, 2-1 weight4, 1-3 weight2 = 9. Yes. Output should be 9. Correction: diameter=9.

DFS: Node4: h=0, Node5: h=0, Node3: heights: 0+2=2, 0+3=3. Top2: [3,2], sum=5. diameter=5. Node3 height=3. Node2: h=0. Node1: heights: 0+5=5 (from 2), 3+1=4 (from 3). Top2: [5,4], sum=9. diameter=max(5,9)=9. Path 2-1-3-5: 5+1+3=9. Wait, but path 4-3-5: 2+3=5 within subtree. Actually check path 2-1-3-4: 5+1+2=8. So 9 is correct. But let's verify if longer exists: 2-1-3-5 is 5+1+3=9. Yes.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Postorder DFS Height Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the longest path from a node to any leaf in its subtree (height) using DFS.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core DFS function that computes for each node its 'height'—the maximum distance from that node to any leaf in its subtree. Intuition: In tree DP, we solve subproblems bottom-up. The height of a node depends on heights of its children plus the edge weights. By computing this recursively, we build the solution from leaves upward. Algorithm: 1. Base case: If node is leaf, return 0. 2. For each child, recursively compute child_height = dfs(child). 3. For each child, compute candidate_height = child_height + edge_weight. 4. Return max(candidate_heights, default=0). Time: O(n) as each node processed once. Space: O(h) recursion depth. Connection: This height is used in L0 to compute diameter via top2 heights. Common mistakes: Not adding edge weight, not handling empty children list, using preorder instead of postorder. Real pattern: def height(node): if not node.children: return 0; return max(height(child) + weight for child,weight in node.children).

examples.trace: DFS: Node2: leaf, height=0. Node3: leaf, height=0. Node1: heights from children: 0+4=4 (from 2), 0+2=2 (from 3). Max=4. So height(1)=4.
DFS: Node3: leaf, h=0. Node4: leaf, h=0. Node2: heights: 0+2=2, 0+3=3. Max=3. height(2)=3. Node1: heights: 3+1=4. Max=4. height(1)=4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Diameter via Two Longest Child Paths
ladders.desc: At a node, compute diameter passing through it as sum of two largest child heights plus their edge weights.
ladders.explanation: The central insight: The diameter of the tree that passes through a given node is the sum of the two longest paths from that node down to leaves in different subtrees. Intuition: Imagine the node as a connecting point—the longest path through it must go down two different branches to maximize distance. If we only take one branch, it's just a height, not a diameter. By summing the top two, we get the best path using this node as the highest point. Algorithm: 1. For a node, collect heights from all children: h_i = child_height_i + edge_weight_i. 2. Find the two largest values among h_i. 3. Diameter through node = sum of these two values (if less than two children, use 0 for missing). 4. Update global diameter if this sum is larger. Time: O(degree) per node. Space: O(1) extra. Connection: This logic uses heights from L1 to compute local diameter contribution. Common mistakes: Not adding edge weights to child heights, not handling nodes with 0 or 1 child, incorrectly selecting top2 (e.g., using sorting instead of O(k) selection). Real pattern: heights = [h+weight for h,weight]; top2 = nlargest(2, heights); diameter = max(diameter, sum(top2)).

examples.input: Node with child heights: [2,5,3] and edge weights [1,1,1] (so h_i = 3,6,4).
examples.trace: Heights after adding weights: 2+1=3, 5+1=6, 3+1=4. Two largest: 6 and 4. Sum=10. So diameter contribution=10.

examples.input: Node with one child: height=7, edge weight=2.
examples.trace: Only one child: heights list = [7+2=9]. Two largest: 9 and 0 (since less than two children, second is 0). Sum=9+0=9. Diameter=9.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal: Postorder DFS
ladders.desc: Traverse a tree using depth-first search in postorder (children before parent).
ladders.explanation: Postorder DFS is a traversal method where we visit all children of a node before visiting the node itself. Intuition: This ensures that when we process a node, we have already computed all necessary information from its subtree, which is essential for bottom-up DP. Algorithm: 1. Start at root. 2. For each child, recursively perform postorder DFS. 3. After visiting all children, process the current node (e.g., compute height). Pseudocode: def postorder(node): for child in node.children: postorder(child); process(node). Time: O(n) visits each node once. Space: O(h) for recursion stack. Connection: This traversal order enables the DP computation in L2 and L1. Common mistakes: Using preorder (parent before children) which doesn't give child info, not handling cycles (but tree has none), iterative implementation complexity. Real pattern: def dfs(node): for child in children: dfs(child); # process node here.

examples.input: Tree: 1-2, 1-3. Root=1.
examples.trace: Start at 1. First child 2: visit 2 (leaf, no children), process 2. Back to 1. Second child 3: visit 3, process 3. Back to 1. Now process 1. Order: 2,3,1.
Start 1. Child 2: go to 2. Child 3: visit 3, process 3. Back to 2. Child 4: visit 4, process 4. Back to 2. Process 2. Back to 1. Process 1. Order: 3,4,2,1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Two Largest Numbers in a List
ladders.desc: Given a list of numbers, efficiently find the two largest values.
ladders.explanation: This operation is needed to compute the diameter through a node by summing the two largest child heights. Intuition: We need the top two values to maximize the sum, but we don't need full sorting—just the two largest. Algorithm: 1. Initialize first_max = -inf, second_max = -inf. 2. For each number in list: if num > first_max: second_max = first_max; first_max = num; else if num > second_max: second_max = num. 3. Return first_max and second_max. Time: O(n) for list of size n. Space: O(1). Connection: Used in L2 to get top2 heights. Common mistakes: Not initializing properly (e.g., with 0 which fails for negative numbers), not updating second_max correctly when first_max changes, handling empty list. Real pattern: top1, top2 = float('-inf'), float('-inf'); for x in list: if x > top1: top2, top1 = top1, x; elif x > top2: top2 = x.

examples.trace: Init: top1=-inf, top2=-inf. x=3: 3 > -inf, so top2=-inf, top1=3. x=1: 1 > -inf but not >3, check >top2? 1 > -inf, so top2=1. x=4: 4 >3, so top2=3, top1=4. x=2: 2 not >4, check >3? no. So top1=4, top2=3.

examples.input: List: [5]
examples.trace: Init: top1=-inf, top2=-inf. x=5: 5 > -inf, so top2=-inf, top1=5. Only one element, so top1=5, top2 remains -inf.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Tree Height and Depth
ladders.desc: Understand the basic concepts of tree height (longest path to leaf) and depth (distance from root).
ladders.explanation: In tree data structures, height of a node is the number of edges on the longest downward path from that node to a leaf. Depth of a node is the number of edges from the root to that node. Intuition: Height measures how 'tall' the subtree is from a node, while depth measures how 'deep' it is from the root. These are fundamental for tree algorithms. For a leaf node, height=0. For the root, depth=0. Algorithm to compute height recursively: height(node) = 1 + max(height(child)) for each child, if any; else 0. Algorithm for depth: depth(node) = depth(parent) + 1. Time: O(n) to compute for all nodes. Space: O(h) for recursion. Connection: Height is the key metric used in L1 and above for DP. Common mistakes: Confusing height with depth, counting nodes instead of edges, not handling empty tree. Real pattern: def height(node): if node is None: return -1; return 1 + max(height(child) for child in node.children).

examples.input: Tree: 1-2, 1-3. Root=1.
examples.trace: Node2: leaf, height=0. Node3: leaf, height=0. Node1: height = 1 + max(0,0) = 1. Depth: root 1 depth=0. Node2 depth=0+1=1. Node3 depth=0+1=1.

examples.input: Tree: 1-2, 2-3. Root=1.
examples.trace: Node3: leaf, height=0. Node2: height = 1 + max(0) = 1. Node1: height = 1 + max(1) = 2. Depth: root 1 depth=0. Node2 depth=1. Node3 depth=2.
